#Text=Of the Food of Children.
#Text=Nature not only points out the food proper for an infant, but actually prepares it.
#Text=This, however, is not sufficient to prevent some who think themselves wiser than Nature, from attempting to bring up their children without her provision.
#Text=Nothing can shew the disposition which mankind have to depart from Nature, more than their endeavouring to bring up children without the breast.
#Text=The mother’s milk, or that of a healthy nurse, is unquestionably the best food for an infant.
#Text=Neither art nor nature can afford a proper substitute for it.
#Text=Children may seem to thrive for a few months without the breast; but, when teething, the small-pox, and other diseases incident to childhood, come on, they generally perish.
#Text=A child, soon after the birth, shews an inclination to suck; and there is no reason why it should not be gratified.

#Text=It is true, the mother’s milk does not always come immediately after the birth; but this is the way to bring it: besides, the first milk that the child can squeeze out of the breast answers the purpose of cleansing, better than all the drugs in the apothecary’s shop, and at the same time prevents inflammations of the breast, fevers, and other diseases incident to mothers.
#Text=It is strange how people came to think that the first thing given to a child should be drugs.
#Text=This is beginning with medicine by times, and no wonder if they generally ended with it.

#Text=It sometimes happens, indeed, that a child does not discharge the meconium so soon as could be wished; this has induced physicians, in such cases, to give something of an opening nature to cleanse the first passages.
#Text=Midwives have improved upon this hint, and never fail to give syrups, oils, &c. whether they be necessary or not.
#Text=Cramming an infant with such indigestible stuff as soon as it is born, can hardly fail to make it sick, and is more likely to occasion diseases, than to prevent them.
#Text=Children are seldom long after the birth without having passage both by stool and urine; though these evacuations may be wanting for some time without any danger.

#Text=But if children must have something before they be allowed the breast, let it be a little thin water-pap, to which may be added an equal quantity of new milk; or rather water alone, with the addition of a little raw sugar.
#Text=If this be given without any wines or spiceries, it will neither heat the blood, load the stomach, nor occasion gripes.
#Text=Upon the first sight of an infant, almost every person is struck with the idea of its being weak, feeble, and wanting support.
#Text=This naturally suggests the need of cordials.
#Text=Accordingly wines are universally mixed with the first food of children.
#Text=Nothing can be more fallacious than this way of reasoning, or more hurtful to infants than the conduct founded upon it.
#Text=Children require very little food for some time after the birth; and what they receive should be thin, weak, light, and of a cooling quality.

#Text=A very small quantity of wine is sufficient to heat and inflame the blood of an infant; but every person conversant in these matters must know, that most of the diseases of infants proceed from the heat of their humours.
#Text=If the mother or nurse has enough of milk, the child will need little or no other food before the third or four month.
#Text=It will then be proper to give it, once or twice a day, a little of some food that is easy of digestion, as water-pap, milk-pottage, weak broth with bread in it, and such like.
#Text=This will ease the mother, will accustom the child by degrees to take food, and will render the weaning both less difficult and less dangerous.
#Text=All great and sudden transitions are to be avoided in nursing.
#Text=For this purpose, the food of children ought not only to be simple, but to resemble, as nearly as possible, the properties of milk.
#Text=Indeed milk itself should make a principal part of their food, not only before they are weaned, but for some time after.
#Text=Next to milk, we would recommend good light bread.
#Text=Bread may be given to a child as soon as it shews an inclination to chew; and it may at all times be allowed as much plain bread as it will eat.
#Text=The very chewing of bread will promote the cutting of the teeth, and the discharge of saliva, while, by mixing with the nurse’s milk in the stomach, it will afford an excellent nourishment.
#Text=Children discover an early inclination to chew whatever is put into their hands.
#Text=Parents observe the inclination, but generally mistake the object.
#Text=Instead of giving the child something which may at once exercise its gums and afford it nourishment, they commonly put into its hands a piece of hard metal, or impenetrable coral.
#Text=A crust of bread is the best gum-stick.

#Text=It not only answers the purpose better than any thing else, but has the additional properties of nourishing the child and carrying the saliva down to the stomach, which is too valuable a liquor to be lost.
#Text=Bread, besides being used dry, may be many ways prepared into food for children.
#Text=One of the best methods is to boil it in water, afterwards pouring the water off, and mixing with the bread a proper quantity of new milk unboiled.
#Text=Milk is both more wholesome and nourishing this way than boiled, and is less apt to occasion costiveness.
#Text=For a child farther advanced, bread may be mixed in veal or chicken broth, made into puddings, or the like.

#Text=Bread is a proper food for children at all times, provided it be plain, made of wholesome grain, and well fermented; but when enriched with fruits, sugars, or such things, it becomes very unwholesome.
#Text=It is soon enough to allow children animal food when they have got teeth to eat it.
#Text=They should never taste it till after they are weaned, and even then they ought to use it sparingly.

#Text=Indeed, when children live wholly on vegetable food, it is apt to sour on their stomachs; but, on the other hand, too much flesh heats the body, and occasions fevers and other inflammatory diseases.
#Text=This plainly points out a due mixture of animal and vegetable food as most proper for children.
#Text=Few things prove more hurtful to infants, than the common method of sweetening their food.
#Text=It entices them to take more than they ought to do, which makes them grow fat and bloated.
#Text=It is pretty certain, if the food of children were quite plain, that they would never take more than enough.
#Text=Their excesses are entirely owing to nurses.

#Text=If a child be gorged with food at all hours, and enticed to take it, by making it sweet and agreeable to the palate, is it any wonder that such a child should in time be induced to crave more food than it ought to have?
#Text=Children may be hurt by too little as well as too much food.
#Text=After a child is weaned, it ought to be fed four or five times a day; but should never be accustomed to eat in the night; neither should it have too much at a time.
#Text=Children thrive best with small quantities of food frequently given.
#Text=This neither overloads the stomach nor hurts the digestion, and is certainly most agreeable to Nature.

#Text=Writers on nursing have inveighed with such vehemence against giving children too much food, that many parents, by endeavouring to shun that error, have run into the opposite extreme, and ruined the constitutions of their children.
#Text=But the error of pinching children in their food is more hurtful than the other extreme.
#Text=Nature has many ways of relieving herself when overcharged; but a child, who is pinched with hunger, will never become a strong or healthy man.
#Text=That errors are frequently committed on both sides, we are ready to acknowledge; but where one child is hurt by the quantity of its food, ten suffer from the quality.
#Text=This is the principal evil, and claims our strictest attention.
#Text=Many people imagine, that the food which they themselves love cannot be bad for their children: but this notion is very absurd.
#Text=In the more advanced periods of life we often acquire an inclination for food, which when children we could not endure.
#Text=Besides, there are many things that by habit may agree very well with the stomach of a grown person, which would be hurtful to a child; as high-seasoned, salted, and smoke-dried provisions, &c.
#Text=It would also be improper to feed children with fat meat, strong broths, rich soups, or the like.
#Text=All strong liquors are hurtful to children.
#Text=Some parents teach their children to guzzle ale, and other fermented liquors, at every meal.
#Text=Such a practice cannot fail to do mischief.
#Text=These children seldom escape the violence of the small-pox, measles, hooping cough, or some inflammatory disorder.
#Text=Milk, water, butter-milk, or whey, are the most proper for children to drink.
#Text=If they have any thing stronger, it may be fine small beer, or a little wine mixed with water.
#Text=The stomachs of children can digest well enough without the assistance of warm stimulants: besides, being naturally hot, they are easily hurt by every thing of a heating quality.
#Text=Few things are more hurtful to children than unripe fruits.
#Text=They weaken the powers of digestion, and sour and relax the stomach, by which means it becomes a proper nest for insects.
#Text=Children indeed shew a great inclination for fruit, and I am apt to believe, that if good ripe fruit were allowed them in proper quantity, it would have no bad effects.
#Text=We never find a natural inclination wrong, if properly regulated.
#Text=Fruits are generally of a cooling nature, and correct the heat and acrimony of the humours.
#Text=This is what most children require; only care should be taken lest they exceed.
#Text=Indeed the best way to prevent children from going to excess in the use of fruit, or eating that which is bad, is to allow them a proper quantity of what is good[12].
#Text=Roots which contain a crude viscid juice should be sparingly given to children.
#Text=They fill the body with gross humours, and tend to produce eruptive diseases.

#Text=This caution is peculiarly necessary for the poor; glad to obtain at a small price what will fill the bellies of their children, they stuff them two or three times a day with greasy potatoes, or other crude vegetables.
#Text=Children had better eat a smaller quantity of food which yields a wholesome nourishment, than be crammed with what their digestive powers are unable properly to assimilate.
#Text=Butter ought likewise to be sparingly given to children.
#Text=It both relaxes the stomach, and produces gross humours.
#Text=Indeed, most things that are fat or oily, have this effect.
#Text=Butter, when salted, becomes still more hurtful.
#Text=Instead of butter, so liberally given to children in most parts of Britain, we would recommend honey.
#Text=Honey is not only wholesome, but cooling, cleansing, and tends to sweeten the humours.
#Text=Children who eat honey are seldom troubled with worms: they are also less subject to cutaneous diseases, as itch, scabbed head, &c.
#Text=Many people err in thinking that the diet of children ought to be altogether moist.
#Text=When children live entirely upon slops, it relaxes their solids, renders them weak, and disposes them to the rickets, the scrophula, and other glandular disorders.
#Text=Relaxation is one of the most general causes of the diseases of children.
#Text=Every thing therefore which tends to unbrace their solids, ought to be carefully avoided.
#Text=We would not be understood by these observations as confining children to any particular kind of food.
#Text=Their diet may be frequently varied, provided always that sufficient regard be had to simplicity.
#Text=OF ALIMENT.
#Text=Unwholesome food, and irregularities in diet, occasion many diseases.
#Text=There is no doubt but the whole constitution of body may be changed by diet alone.
#Text=The fluids may be thereby attenuated or condensed, rendered mild or acrimonious, coagulated or diluted, to almost any degree.
#Text=Nor are its effects upon the solids less considerable.
#Text=They may be braced or relaxed, have their sensibility, motions, &c. greatly increased or diminished, by different kinds of aliment.
#Text=A very small attention to these things will be sufficient to shew, how much the preservation of health depends upon a proper regimen of the diet.
#Text=Nor is an attention to diet necessary for the preservation of health only: it is likewise of importance in the cure of diseases.
#Text=Every intention in the cure of many diseases, may be answered by diet alone.

#Text=Its effects, indeed, are not always so quick as those of medicine, but they are generally more lasting: besides, it is neither so disagreeable to the patient, nor so dangerous as medicine, and is always more easily obtained.

#Text=Our intention here is not to inquire minutely into the nature and properties of the various kinds of aliment in use among mankind; nor to shew their effects upon the different constitutions of the human body; but to mark some of the most pernicious errors which people are apt to fall into, with respect both to the quantity and qualities of their food, and to point out their influence upon health.
#Text=It is not indeed an easy matter to ascertain the exact quantity of food proper for every age, sex, and constitution: but a scrupulous nicety here is by no means necessary.
#Text=The best rule is to avoid all extremes.
#Text=Mankind were never intended to weigh and measure their food.
#Text=Nature teaches every creature when it has enough; and the calls of thirst and hunger are sufficient to inform them when more is necessary.
#Text=Though moderation is the chief rule with regard to the quantity, yet the quality of food merits a farther consideration.
#Text=There are many ways by which provisions may be rendered unwholesome.
#Text=Bad seasons may either prevent the ripening of grain, or damage it afterwards.

#Text=These, indeed, are acts of Providence, and we must submit to them; but surely no punishment can be too severe for those who suffer provisions to spoil by hoarding them, on purpose to raise the price, or who promote their own interest by adulterating the necessaries of life[27].
#Text=Animal, as well as vegetable food, may be rendered unwholesome, by being kept too long.
#Text=All animal substances have a constant tendency to putrefaction; and, when that has proceeded too far, they not only become offensive to the senses, but hurtful to health.
#Text=Diseased animals, and such as die of themselves, ought never to be eaten.
#Text=It is a common practice, however, in some grazing countries, for servants and poor people to eat such animals as die of any disease, or are killed by accident.

#Text=Poverty, indeed, may oblige people to do this; but they had better eat a smaller quantity of what is sound and wholesome: it would both afford a better nourishment, and be attended with less danger.
#Text=The injunctions given to the Jews, not to eat any creature which died of itself, seem to have a strict regard to health; and ought to be observed by Christians as well as Jews.

#Text=Animals never die themselves without some previous disease; but how a diseased animal should be wholesome food, is inconceivable: even those which die by accident must be hurtful, as their blood is mixed with the flesh, and soon turns putrid.
#Text=Animals which feed grossly, as tame ducks, hogs, &c. are neither so easily digested, nor afford such wholesome nourishment as others.
#Text=No animal can be wholesome which does not take sufficient exercise.

#Text=Most of our stalled cattle are crammed with gross food, but not allowed exercise nor free air; by which means they indeed grow fat, but their juices not being properly prepared or assimilated, remain crude, and occasion indigestions, gross humours, and oppression of the spirits, in those who feed upon them.
#Text=Animals are often rendered unwholesome by being overheated.
#Text=Excessive heat causes a fever, exalts the animal salts, and mixes the blood so intimately with the flesh, that it cannot be separated.
#Text=For this reason, butchers should be severely punished who over-drive their cattle.
#Text=No person would chuse to eat the flesh of an animal which had died in a high fever; yet that is the case with all over-drove cattle; and the fever is often raised even to the degree of madness.
#Text=But this is not the only way by which butchers render meat unwholesome.
#Text=The abominable custom of filling the cellular membrane of animals with air, in order to make them appear fat, is every day practised.

#Text=This not only spoils the meat, and renders it unfit for keeping, but is such a dirty trick, that the very idea of it is sufficient to disgust a person of any delicacy at every thing which comes from the shambles.
#Text=Who can bear the thought of eating meat which has been blown up with air from the lungs of a dirty fellow, perhaps labouring under the very worst of diseases?
#Text=Butchers have likewise a method of filling the cellular membranes of animals with blood.
#Text=This makes the meat seem fatter, and likewise weigh more, but is notwithstanding a very pernicious custom, as it both renders the meat unwholesome and unfit for keeping.
#Text=I seldom see a piece of meat from the shambles, where the blood is not diffused through the cellular texture.
#Text=I shall not say that this is always the effect of design; but I am certain it is not the case with animals that are killed for domestic use, and properly blooded.
#Text=Veal seems to be most frequently spoilt in this way.
#Text=Perhaps that may in some measure be owing to the practice of carrying calves from a great distance to market, by which means their tender flesh is bruised, and many of their vessels burst.

#Text=No people in the world eat such quantities of animal food as the English, which is one reason why they are so generally tainted with the scurvy and its numerous train of consequences, indigestion, low spirits, hypochondriacism, &c.

#Text=Animal food was surely designed for man, and, with a proper mixture of vegetables, it will be found the most wholesome; but to gorge beef, mutton, pork, fish, and fowl, twice or thrice a day, is certainly too much.
#Text=All who value health ought to be contented with making one meal of flesh in the twenty-four hours, and this ought to consist of one kind only.
#Text=The most obstinate scurvy has often been cured by a vegetable diet; nay, milk alone will frequently do more in that disease than any medicine.
#Text=Hence it is evident, that if vegetables and milk were more used in diet, we should have less scurvy, and likewise fewer putrid and inflammatory fevers.
#Text=Fresh vegetables, indeed, come to be daily more used in diet; this laudable practice we hope will continue to gain ground.
#Text=Our aliment ought neither to be too moist nor too dry.
#Text=Moist aliment relaxes the solids, and renders the body feeble.
#Text=Thus we see females, who live much on tea and other watery diet, generally become weak, and unable to digest solid food: hence proceed hysterics, and all their dreadful consequences.
#Text=On the other hand, food that is too dry, renders the solids in a manner rigid, and the humours viscid, which disposes the body to inflammatory fevers, scurvies, and the like.
#Text=Much has been said on the ill effects of tea in diet.
#Text=They are, no doubt, numerous; but they proceed rather from the imprudent use of it, than from any bad qualities in the tea itself.
#Text=Tea is now the universal breakfast in this part of the world; but the morning is surely the most improper time of the day for drinking it.
#Text=Most delicate persons, who, by the bye, are the greatest tea-drinkers, cannot eat any thing in the morning.
#Text=If such persons, after fasting ten or twelve hours, drink four or five cups of tea without eating almost any bread, it must hurt them.

#Text=Good tea, taken in moderate quantity, not too strong, nor too hot, nor drank upon an empty stomach, will seldom do harm; but if it be bad, which is often the case, or substituted in the room of solid food, it must have many ill effects.
#Text=The arts of cookery render many things unwholesome, which are not so in their own nature.
#Text=By jumbling together a number of different ingredients, in order to make a poignant sauce or rich soup, the composition proves almost a poison.
#Text=All high seasoning, pickles, &c. are only incentives to luxury, and never fail to hurt the stomach.
#Text=It were well for mankind, if cookery, as an art, were entirely prohibited.
#Text=Plain roasting or boiling is all that the stomach requires.
#Text=These alone are sufficient for people in health, and the sick have still less need of a cook.
#Text=The liquid part of our aliment likewise claims our attention.
#Text=Water is not only the basis of most liquors, but also composes a great part of our solid food.
#Text=Good water must therefore be of the greatest importance in diet.
#Text=The best water is that which is most pure, and free from any mixture of foreign bodies.
#Text=Water takes up parts of most bodies with which it comes into contact; by this means it is often impregnated with metals or minerals of a hurtful or poisonous nature.
#Text=Hence the inhabitants of some hilly countries have peculiar diseases, which in all probability proceed from the water.
#Text=Thus the people who live near the Alps in Switzerland, and the inhabitants of the Peak of Derby in England, have large tumours or wens on their necks.
#Text=This disease is generally imputed to the snow water; but there is more reason to believe it is owing to the minerals in the mountains through which the waters pass.
#Text=When water is impregnated with foreign bodies, it generally appears by its weight, colour, taste, smell, heat, or some other sensible quality.
#Text=Our business therefore is to chuse such water, for common use, as is lightest, and without any particular colour, taste, or smell.
#Text=In most places of Britain the inhabitants have it in their power to make choice of their water; and few things would contribute more to health than a due attention to this article.
#Text=But mere indolence often induces people to make use of the water that is nearest to them, without considering its qualities.

#Text=Before water is brought into great towns, the strictest attention ought to be paid to its qualities, as many diseases may be occasioned or aggravated by bad water; and when once it has been procured at a great expence, people are unwilling to give it up.
#Text=The common methods of rendering water clear by filtration, or soft, by exposing it to the sun and air, &c. are so generally known, that it is unnecessary to spend time in explaining them.

#Text=We shall only, in general, advise all to avoid waters which stagnate long in small lakes, ponds, or the like, as such waters often become putrid, by the corruption of animal and vegetable bodies with which they abound.
#Text=Even cattle frequently suffer by drinking, in dry seasons, water which has stood long in small reservoirs, without being supplied by springs, or freshened with showers.
#Text=All wells ought to be kept clean, and to have a free communication with the air.

#Text=As fermented liquors, notwithstanding they have been exclaimed against by many writers, still continue to be the common drink of almost every person who can afford them; we shall rather endeavour to assist people in the choice of these liquors, than pretend to condemn what custom has so firmly established.
#Text=It is not the moderate use of sound fermented liquors which hurts mankind: it is excess, and using such as are ill prepared or vitiated.
#Text=Fermented liquors, which are too strong, hurt digestion; and the body is so far from being strengthened by them, that it is weakened and relaxed.
#Text=Many imagine that hard labour could not be supported without drinking strong liquors: this is a very erroneous notion.
#Text=Men who never taste strong liquors are not only able to endure more fatigue, but also live much longer, than those who use them daily.
#Text=But, suppose strong liquors did enable a man to do more work, they must nevertheless waste the powers of life, and occasion premature old age.
#Text=They keep up a constant fever, which exhausts the spirits, inflames the blood, and disposes the body to numberless diseases.

#Text=But fermented liquors may be too weak as well as too strong: when that is the case, they must either be drank new, or they become sour and dead: when such liquors are drank new, the fermentation not being over, they generate air in the bowels, and occasion flatulencies; and, when kept till stale, they turn sour on the stomach, and hurt digestion.
#Text=For this reason all malt liquors, cider, &c. ought to be of such strength as to keep till they be ripe, and then they should be used.
#Text=When such liquors are kept too long, though they should not become sour, yet they generally contract a hardness, which renders them unwholesome.
#Text=All families, who can, ought to prepare their own liquors.
#Text=Since preparing and vending of liquors became one of the most general branches of business, every method has been tried to adulterate them.
#Text=The great object both to the makers and venders of liquor is, to render it intoxicating, and give it the appearance of age.
#Text=But it is well known that this may be done by other ingredients than those which ought to be used for making it strong.
#Text=It would be imprudent even to name those things which are daily made use of to render liquors heady.
#Text=Suffice it to say, that the practice is very common, and that all the ingredients used for this purpose are of a narcotic or stupefactive quality.
#Text=But as all opiates are poisonous, it is easy to see what must be the consequence of their general use.
#Text=Though they do not kill suddenly, yet they hurt the nerves, relax and weaken the stomach, and spoil the digestion.
#Text=Were fermented liquors faithfully prepared, kept to a proper age, and used in moderation, they would prove real blessings to mankind.
#Text=But, while they are ill prepared, various ways adulterated, and taken to excess, they must have many pernicious effects.
#Text=We would recommend it to families, not only to prepare their own liquors, but likewise their bread.
#Text=Bread is so necessary a part of diet, that too much care cannot be bestowed in order to have it sound and wholesome.
#Text=For this purpose, it is not only necessary that it be made of good grain, but likewise properly prepared, and kept free from all unwholesome ingredients.
#Text=This, however, we have reason to believe is not always the case with bread prepared by those who make a trade of vending it.
#Text=Their object is rather to please the eye, than to consult the health.
#Text=The best bread is that which is neither too coarse nor too fine; well fermented and made of wheat flour, or rather of wheat and rye mixed together.
#Text=To specify the different kinds of aliment, to explain their nature and properties, and to point out their effects in different constitutions, would far exceed the limits of our design.

#Text=Instead of a detail of this kind, which would not be generally understood, and of course little attended to, we shall only mention the following easy rules with respect to the choice of aliment.
#Text=Persons, whose solids are weak and relaxed, ought to avoid all viscid food, or such things as are hard of digestion.
#Text=Their diet, however, ought to be nourishing; and they should take sufficient exercise in the open air.
#Text=Such as abound with blood should be sparing in the use of every thing that is highly nourishing, as fat meat, rich wines, strong ale, and such like.
#Text=Their food should consist chiefly of bread and other vegetable substances; and their drink ought to be water, whey, or small beer.
#Text=Fat people should not eat freely of oily nourishing diet.
#Text=They ought frequently to use raddish, garlic, spices, or such things as are heating and promote perspiration and urine.
#Text=Their drink should be water, coffee, tea, or the like; and they ought to take much exercise and little sleep.
#Text=Those who are too lean must follow an opposite course.

#Text=Such as are troubled with acidities, or whose food is apt to sour on the stomach, should live much on animal food; and those who are afflicted with hot alkaline eructations, ought to use a diet consisting chiefly of acid vegetables.

#Text=People who are affected with the gout, low spirits, hypochondriac or hysteric disorders, ought to avoid all flatulent food, every thing that is viscid, or hard of digestion, all salted or smoke-dried provisions, and whatever is austere, acid, or apt to turn sour on the stomach.
#Text=Their food should be light, spare, cool, and of an opening nature.

#Text=The diet ought not only to be suited to the age and constitution, but also to the manner of life: a sedentary or studious person should live more sparingly than one who labours hard without doors.
#Text=Many kinds of food will nourish a peasant very well which would be almost indigestible to a citizen; and the latter will live upon a diet on which the former would starve.
#Text=Diet ought not to be too uniform.
#Text=The constant use of one kind of food might have some bad effects.
#Text=Nature teaches us this, by the great variety of aliment which she has provided for man, and likewise by giving him an appetite for different kinds of food.

#Text=Those who labour under any particular disease, ought to avoid such aliments as have a tendency to increase it: for example, a gouty person should not indulge in rich wines, strong soups, or gravies, and should avoid all acids.
#Text=One who is troubled with the gravel ought to shun all austere and astringent aliments; and those who are scorbutic should be sparing in the use of salted provisions, &c.
#Text=In the first period of life, our food ought to be light, but nourishing, and frequently taken.
#Text=Food that is solid, with a sufficient degree of tenacity, is most proper for the state of manhood.
#Text=The diet suited to the last period of life, when nature is upon the decline, approaches nearly to that of the first.
#Text=It should be lighter and more succulent than that of vigorous age, and likewise more frequently taken.
#Text=It is not only necessary for health that our diet be wholesome, but also that it be taken at regular periods.
#Text=Some imagine long fasting will atone for excess; but this, instead of mending the matter, generally makes it worse.
#Text=When the stomach and intestines are over-distended with food, they lose their proper tone, and, by long fasting, they become weak, and inflated with wind.
#Text=Thus, either gluttony or fasting destroys the powers of digestion.
#Text=The frequent repetition of aliment is not only necessary for repairing the continual waste of our bodies, but likewise to keep the fluids sound and sweet.

#Text=Our humours, even in the most healthy state, have a constant tendency to putrefaction, which can only be prevented by frequent supplies of fresh nourishment: when that is wanting too long, the putrefaction often proceeds so far as to occasion very dangerous fevers.
#Text=From hence we may learn the necessity of regular meals.
#Text=No person can enjoy a good state of health, whose vessels are either frequently overcharged, or the humours long deprived of fresh supplies of chyle.
#Text=Long fasting is extremely hurtful to young people; it not only vitiates their humours, but prevents their growth.
#Text=Nor is it less injurious to the aged.
#Text=Most persons, in the decline of life, are afflicted with wind: this complaint is not only increased, but even rendered dangerous, and often fatal, by long fasting.
#Text=Old people, when their stomachs are empty, are frequently seized with giddiness, head-achs, and faintness.
#Text=These complaints may generally be removed by a piece of bread and a glass of wine, or taking any other solid food; which plainly points out the method of preventing them.

#Text=It is more than probable, that many of the sudden deaths, which happen in the advanced periods of life, are occasioned by fasting too long, as it exhausts the spirits, and fills the bowels with wind; we would therefore advise people, in the decline of life, never to allow their stomachs to be too long empty.
#Text=Many people take nothing but a few cups of tea and a little bread, from nine o’clock at night till two or three next afternoon.
#Text=Such may be said to fast almost three-fourths of their time.
#Text=This can hardly fail to ruin the appetite, vitiate the humours, and fill the bowels with wind; all which might be prevented by a solid breakfast.
#Text=It is a very common practice to eat a light breakfast and a heavy supper.
#Text=This custom ought to be reversed.
#Text=When people sup late, their supper should be very light; but the breakfast ought always to be solid.
#Text=If any one eats a light supper, goes soon to bed, and rises betimes in the morning, he will be sure to find an appetite for his breakfast, and he may freely indulge it.
#Text=The strong and healthy do not indeed suffer so much from fasting as the weak and delicate; but they run great hazard from its opposite, viz. repletion.
#Text=Many diseases, especially fevers, are the effect of a plethora, or too great fulness of the vessels.
#Text=Strong people, in high health, have generally a great quantity of blood and other humours.
#Text=When these are suddenly increased, by an overcharge of rich and nourishing diet, the vessels become too much distended, and obstructions and inflammations ensue.
#Text=Hence so many people are seized with inflammatory and eruptive fevers after a feast or debauch.
#Text=All great and sudden changes in diet are dangerous.
#Text=What the stomach has been long accustomed to digest, though less wholesome, will agree better with it than food of a more salutary nature which it has not been used to.

#Text=When therefore a change becomes necessary, it ought always to be made gradually; a sudden transition from a poor and low, to a rich and luxurious diet, or the contrary, might so disturb the functions of the body as to endanger health, or even to occasion death itself.
#Text=When we recommend regularity in diet, we would not be understood as condemning every small deviation from it.
#Text=It is next to impossible for people at all times to avoid some degree of excess, and living too much by rule might make even the smallest deviation dangerous.
#Text=It may therefore be prudent to vary a little, sometimes taking more, sometimes less, than the usual quantity of meat and drink, provided always that a due regard be had to moderation.
#Text=INFLAMMATION OF THE STOMACH, AND OTHER VISCERA.
#Text=All inflammations of the bowels are dangerous, and require the most speedy assistance; as they frequently end in a suppuration, and sometimes in a mortification, which is certain death.
#Text=CAUSES.

#Text=——An inflammation of the stomach may proceed from any of the causes which produce an inflammatory fever; as cold liquor drank while the body is warm, obstructed perspiration, or the sudden striking in of any eruption.
#Text=It may likewise proceed from the acrimony of the bile, or from acrid and stimulating substances taken into the stomach; as strong vomits or purges, corrosive poisons, and such like.
#Text=When the gout has been repelled from the extremities, either by cold or improper applications, it often occasions an inflammation of the stomach.
#Text=Hard or indigestible substances taken into the stomach, as bones, the stones of fruit, &c. may likewise have that effect.
#Text=SYMPTOMS.

#Text=——It is attended with a fixed pain and burning heat in the stomach; great restlessness and anxiety; a small, quick, and hard pulse; vomiting, or, at least, a nausea and sickness; excessive thirst; coldness of the extremities; difficulty of breathing; cold clammy sweats; and sometimes convulsions and fainting fits.
#Text=The stomach is swelled, and often feels hard to the touch.
#Text=One of the most certain signs of this disease is the sense of pain, which the patient feels upon taking any kind of food or drink, especially if it be either too hot or too cold.
#Text=When the patient vomits every thing he eats or drinks, is extremely restless, has a hiccup, with an intermitting pulse, and frequent fainting fits, the danger is very great.
#Text=REGIMEN.
#Text=——All acrimonious, heating, and irritating food and drink are carefully to be avoided.

#Text=The weakness of the patient may deceive the bystanders, and induce them to give him wines, spirits, or other cordials; but these never fail to increase the disease, and often occasion sudden death.
#Text=The inclination to vomit may likewise impose on the attendants, and make them think a vomit necessary; but that too is almost certain death.
#Text=The food must be light, thin, cool, and easy of digestion.
#Text=It must be given in small quantities, and should neither be quite cold nor too hot.
#Text=Thin gruel made of barley or oatmeal, light toasted bread dissolved in boiling water, or very weak chicken broth, are the most proper.
#Text=The drink should be clear whey, barley-water, water in which toasted bread has been boiled, or decoctions of emollient vegetables; as liquorice and marsh-mallow roots, sarsaparilla, &c.
#Text=MEDICINE.
#Text=——Bleeding in this disease is absolutely necessary, and is almost the only thing that can be depended on.
#Text=When the disease proves obstinate, it will often be proper to repeat this operation several times, nor must the low state of the pulse deter us from doing so.
#Text=The pulse indeed generally rises upon bleeding, and as long as that is the case, the operation is safe.
#Text=Frequent fomentations with lukewarm water, or a decoction of emollient vegetables, are likewise beneficial.
#Text=Flannel cloths dipped in these must be applied to the region of the stomach, and removed as they grow cool.
#Text=They must neither be applied too warm, nor be suffered to continue till they become quite cold, as either of these extremities would aggravate the disease.
#Text=The feet and legs ought likewise to be frequently bathed in lukewarm water, and warm bricks or poultices may be applied to the soles of the feet.
#Text=The warm bath, if it can be conveniently used, will be of great service.
#Text=In this, and all other inflammations of the bowels, an epispastic, or blistering-plaster, applied over the part affected, is one of the best remedies I know.
#Text=I have often used it, and do not recollect one instance wherein it did not give relief to the patient.
#Text=The only internal medicines which we shall venture to recommend in this disease, are mild clysters.
#Text=These may be made of warm water, or thin water-gruel; and if the patient is costive, a little sweet oil, honey, or manna, may be added.

#Text=Clysters answer the purpose of an internal fomentation, while they keep the body open, and at the same time nourish the patient, who is often in this disease unable to retain any food upon his stomach.
#Text=For these reasons they must not be neglected, as the patient’s life may depend on them.
#Text=INFLAMMATION OF THE INTESTINES.
#Text=This is one of the most painful and dangerous diseases that mankind is liable to.

#Text=It generally proceeds from the same causes as the inflammation of the stomach; to which may be added costiveness, worms, eating unripe fruits, or great quantities of nuts, drinking hard windy malt liquors, as stale bottled beer or ale, sour wine, cyder, &c.
#Text=It may likewise be occasioned by a rupture, by schirrous tumours of the intestines, or by their opposite sides growing together.
#Text=The inflammation of the intestines is denominated Iliac passion, Enteritis, &c. according to the name of the parts affected.
#Text=The treatment however is nearly the same whatever part of the intestinal canal be the seat of the disease; we shall therefore omit these distinctions, lest they should perplex the reader.
#Text=The symptoms here are nearly the same as in the foregoing disease; only the pain, if possible, is more acute, and is situated lower.
#Text=The vomiting is likewise more violent, and sometimes even the excrements, together with the clysters, are discharged by the mouth.
#Text=The patient is continually belching up wind, and has often an obstruction of his urine.

#Text=While the pain shifts, and the vomiting only returns at certain intervals, and while the clysters pass downwards, there is ground for hope; but when the clysters and fæces are vomited, and the patient is exceeding weak, with a low fluttering pulse, a pale countenance, and a disagreeable or stinking breath, there is great reason to fear that the consequences will prove fatal.
#Text=Clammy sweats, black fœtid stools, with a small intermitting pulse, and a total cessation of pain, are signs of a mortification already begun, and of approaching death.
#Text=REGIMEN.
#Text=——The regimen in this disease is in general the same as in an inflammation of the stomach.
#Text=The patient must be kept quiet, avoiding cold, and all violent passions of the mind.
#Text=His food ought to be very light, and given in small quantities; his drink weak and diluting; as clear whey, barley-water, and such like.
#Text=MEDICINE.
#Text=——Bleeding in this, as well as in the inflammation of the stomach, is of the greatest importance.
#Text=It should be performed as soon as the symptoms appear, and must be repeated according to the strength of the patient, and the violence of the disease.
#Text=A blistering plaster is here likewise to be applied immediately over the part where the most violent pain is.
#Text=This not only relieves the pain of the bowels, but even clysters and purgative medicines, which before had no effect, will operate when the blister begins to rise.
#Text=Fomentations and laxative clysters are by no means to be omitted.
#Text=The patient’s feet and legs should frequently be bathed in warm water; and cloths dipped in it applied to his belly.
#Text=Bladders filled with warm water may likewise be applied to the region of the navel, and warm bricks, or bottles filled with warm water, to the soles of the feet.
#Text=The clysters may be made of barley-water or thin gruel with salt, and softened with sweet oil or fresh butter.
#Text=These may be administered every two or three hours, or oftener, if the patient continues costive.

#Text=If the disease does not yield to clysters and fomentations, recourse must be had to pretty strong purgatives; but as these, by irritating the bowels, often increase their contraction, and by that means frustrate their own intention, it will be necessary to join them with opiates, which, by allaying the pain, and relaxing the spasmodic contractions of the guts, greatly assist the operation of purgatives in this case.
#Text=What answers the purpose of opening the body very well, is a solution of the bitter purging salts.
#Text=Two ounces of these may be dissolved in an English pint of warm water, or thin gruel, and a tea-cupful of it taken every half hour till it operates.
#Text=At the same time fifteen, twenty, or twenty-five drops of laudanum may be given in a glass of peppermint or simple cinnamon-water, to appease the irritation, and prevent the vomiting, &c.
#Text=Acids have often a very happy effect in staying the vomiting, and appeasing the other violent symptoms of this disease.
#Text=It will therefore be of use to sharpen the patient’s drink with cream of tartar, juice of lemon; or, when these cannot be obtained, with vinegar.
#Text=But it often happens that no liquid whatever will stay on the stomach.
#Text=In this case the patient must take purging pills.

#Text=I have generally found the following answer very well: Take jalap in powder, and vitriolated tartar, of each half a drachm, opium one grain, Castile soap as much as will make the mass fit for pills.
#Text=These must be taken at one dose, and if they do not operate in a few hours, the dose may be repeated.
#Text=If a stool cannot be procured by any of the above means, it will be necessary to immerse the patient in warm water up to the breast.
#Text=I have often seen this succeed when other means had been tried in vain.

#Text=The patient must continue in the water as long as he can easily bear it without fainting, and if one immersion has not the desired effect, it may be repeated as soon as the patient’s strength and spirits are recruited.
#Text=It is more safe for him to go frequently into the bath, than to continue too long at a time; and it is often necessary to repeat it several times before it has the desired effect.

#Text=It has sometimes happened, after all other means of procuring a stool had been tried to no purpose, that this was brought about by immersing the patient’s lower extremities in cold water, or making him walk upon a wet pavement, and dashing his legs and thighs with the cold water.
#Text=This method, when others fail, at least merits a trial.
#Text=It is indeed attended with some danger; but a doubtful remedy is better than none.
#Text=In desperate cases it is common to give quicksilver.
#Text=This may be given to the quantity of several ounces, or even a pound, but should not exceed that[116].
#Text=When there is reason to suspect a mortification of the guts, this medicine ought not to be tried.
#Text=In that case it cannot cure the patient, and will only hasten his death.

#Text=But when the obstruction is occasioned by any cause that can be removed by force, quicksilver is not only a proper medicine, but the best that can be administered, as it is the fittest body we know for making its way through the intestinal canal.
#Text=If the disease proceed from a rupture, the patient must be laid with his head very low, and the intestines returned by gentle pressure with the hand.
#Text=If this, with fomentations and clysters, should not succeed, recourse must be had to a surgical operation, which may give the patient relief.
#Text=Such as would avoid this excruciating and dangerous disease, must take care never to be too long without a stool.
#Text=Some who have died of it have had several pounds of hard dry fæces taken out of their guts.
#Text=They should likewise beware of eating too freely of sour or unripe fruits, or drinking stale windy liquors, &c.
#Text=I have known it brought on by living too much on baked fruits, which are seldom good.
#Text=It likewise proceeds frequently from cold caught by wet clothes, &c. but especially from wet feet.
#Text=OF THE COLIC.
#Text=The colic has a great resemblance to the two preceding diseases, both in its symptoms and method of cure.
#Text=It is generally attended with costiveness and acute pain of the bowels; and requires diluting diet, evacuations, fomentations, &c.
#Text=Colics are variously denominated according to their causes, as the flatulent, the bilious, the hysteric, the nervous, &c.
#Text=As each of these requires a particular method of treatment, we shall point out their most general symptoms, and the means to be used for their relief.
#Text=The flatulent, or wind-colic, is generally occasioned by an indiscreet use of unripe fruits, meats of hard digestion, windy vegetables, fermenting liquors, and such like.
#Text=It may likewise proceed from an obstructed perspiration, or catching cold.
#Text=Delicate people, whose digestive powers are weak, are most liable to this kind of colic.
#Text=The flatulent colic may either affect the stomach or intestines.
#Text=It is attended with a painful stretching of the affected part.
#Text=The patient feels a rumbling in his guts, and is generally relieved by a discharge of wind, either upwards or downwards.
#Text=The pain is seldom confined to any particular part, as the vapour wanders from one division of the bowels to another till it finds a vent.
#Text=When the disease proceeds from windy liquor, green fruit, sour herbs, or the like, the best medicine on the first appearance of the symptoms is a dram of brandy, gin, or any good spirits.
#Text=The patient should likewise sit with his feet upon a warm hearth-stone, or apply warm bricks to them; and warm cloths may be applied to his stomach and bowels.
#Text=This is the only colic wherein ardent spirits, spiceries, or any thing of a hot nature, may be ventured upon.
#Text=Nor indeed are they to be used here unless at the very beginning, before any symptoms of inflammation appear.

#Text=We have reason to believe, that a colic occasioned by wind or flatulent food might always be cured by spirits and warm liquors, if they were taken immediately upon perceiving the first uneasiness; but when the pain has continued for a considerable time, and there is reason to fear an inflammation of the bowels is already begun, all hot things are to be avoided as poison, and the patient is to be treated in the same manner as for the inflammation of the intestines.
#Text=Several kinds of food, as honey, eggs, &c. occasion colics in some particular constitutions.
#Text=I have generally found the best method of cure for these was to drink plentifully of small diluting liquors, as water-gruel, small posset, water with toasted bread soaked in it, &c.
#Text=Colics which proceed from excess and indigestion generally cure themselves by occasioning vomiting or purging.
#Text=These discharges are by no means to be stopped, but promoted by drinking plentifully of warm water, or weak posset.
#Text=When their violence is over, the patient may take a dose of rhubarb, or any other gentle purge, to carry off the dregs of his debauch.

#Text=Colics which are occasioned by wet feet, or catching cold, may generally be removed at the beginning, by bathing the feet and legs in warm water, and drinking such warm diluting liquors as will promote the perspiration, as weak wine-whey, or water-gruel, with a small quantity of spirits in it.
#Text=Those flatulent colics, which prevail so much among country people, might generally be prevented were they careful to change their clothes when they get wet.
#Text=They ought likewise to take a dram, or to drink some warm liquor after eating any kind of green trash.
#Text=We do not mean to recommend the practice of dram-drinking, but in this case ardent spirits prove a real medicine, and indeed the best that can be administered.
#Text=A glass of good peppermint water will have nearly the same effect as a glass of brandy, and in some cases is rather to be preferred.
#Text=The bilious colic is attended with very acute pains about the region of the navel.
#Text=The patient complains of great thirst, and is generally costive.
#Text=He vomits a hot, bitter, yellow-coloured bile, which being discharged, seems to afford some relief, but is quickly followed by the same violent pain as before.

#Text=As the distemper advances, the propensity to vomit sometimes increases so as to become almost continual, and the proper motion of the intestines is so far perverted, that there are all the symptoms of an impending iliac passion.
#Text=If the patient be young and strong, and the pulse full and frequent, it will be proper to bleed, after which clysters may be administered.
#Text=Clear whey or gruel, sharpened with the juice of lemon, or cream of tartar, must be drank freely.
#Text=Small chicken-broth, with a little manna dissolved in it, or a slight decoction of tamarinds, are likewise very proper, or any other thin, acid, opening liquor.

#Text=Besides bleeding and plentiful dilution, it will be necessary to foment the belly with cloths dipped in warm water, and if this should not succeed, the patient must be immersed up to the breast in warm water.
#Text=In the bilious colic the vomiting is often very difficult to restrain.
#Text=When this happens, the patient may drink a decoction of toasted bread, or an infusion of garden-mint in boiling water.
#Text=Should these not have the desired effect, the saline draught, with a few drops of laudanum in it, may be given, and repeated according to the urgency of the symptoms.
#Text=A small quantity of Venice treacle may be spread in form of a cataplasm, and applied to the pit of the stomach.
#Text=Clysters, with a proper quantity of Venice treacle or liquid laudanum in them, may likewise be frequently administered.
#Text=Such as are liable to frequent returns of the bilious colic should use flesh sparingly, and live chiefly upon a light vegetable diet.
#Text=They should likewise take frequently a dose of cream of tartar with tamarinds, or any other cool acid purge.
#Text=The hysteric colic bears a great resemblance to the bilious.
#Text=It is attended with acute pains about the region of the stomach, vomiting, &c.
#Text=But what the patient vomits in this case is commonly of a greenish colour.
#Text=There is a great sinking of the spirits, with dejection of mind and difficulty of breathing, which are the characteristic symptoms of this disorder.
#Text=Sometimes it is accompanied with the jaundice, but this generally goes off of its own accord in a few days.
#Text=In this colic all evacuations, as bleeding, purging, vomiting, &c. do hurt.
#Text=Every thing that weakens the patient, or sinks the spirits, is to be avoided.
#Text=If however the vomiting should prove violent, lukewarm water, or small posset, may be drank to cleanse the stomach.
#Text=Afterwards the patient may take fifteen, twenty, or twenty-five drops of liquid laudanum in a glass of cinnamon water.
#Text=This may be repeated every ten or twelve hours till the symptoms abate.
#Text=The patient may likewise take four or five of the fœtid pills every six hours, and drink a cup of penny-royal tea after them.

#Text=If asafœtida should prove disagreeable, which is sometimes the case, a tea-spoonful of the tincture of castor in a cup of penny-royal tea, or thirty or forty drops of the balsam of Peru dropped upon a bit of loaf-sugar, may be taken in its stead.
#Text=The anti-hysteric plaster may also be used, which has often a good effect[117].
#Text=The nervous colic prevails among miners, smelters of lead, plumbers, the manufacturers of white lead, &c.
#Text=It is very common in the cyder counties of England, and is supposed to be occasioned by the leaden vessels used in preparing that liquor.
#Text=It is likewise a frequent disease in the West Indies, where it is termed the dry belly-ache.
#Text=No disease of the bowels is attended with more excruciating pain than this.
#Text=Nor is it soon at an end.
#Text=I have known it continue eight or ten days with very little intermission, the body all the while continuing bound in spite of medicine, yet at length yield, and the patient recover[118].
#Text=It generally however leaves the patient weak, and often ends in a palsy.
#Text=The general treatment of this disease is so nearly the same with that of the iliac passion, or inflammation of the guts, that we shall not insist upon it.
#Text=The body is to be opened by mild purgatives given in small doses, and frequently repeated, and their operation must be assisted by soft oily clysters, fomentations, &c.
#Text=The castor oil is reckoned peculiarly proper in this disease.
#Text=It may both be mixed with the clysters and given by the mouth.
#Text=The Barbadoes tar is said to be an efficacious medicine in this complaint.
#Text=It may be taken to the quantity of two drachms three times a day, or oftner if the stomach will bear it.
#Text=This tar, mixed with an equal quantity of strong rum, is likewise proper for rubbing the spine, in case any tingling, or other symptoms of a palsy, are felt.
#Text=When the tar cannot be obtained, the back may be rubbed with strong spirits, or a little oil of nutmegs or of rosemary.
#Text=If the patient remains weak and languid after this disease, he must take exercise on horseback, and use an infusion of the Peruvian bark in wine.
#Text=When the disease ends in a palsy, the Bath-waters are found to be extremely proper.
#Text=To avoid this kind of colic, people must shun all sour fruits, acids, and austere liquors, &c.
#Text=Those who work in lead ought never to go to their business fasting, and their food should be oily or fat.
#Text=They may take a glass of sallad oil, with a little brandy or rum every morning, but should never take spirits alone.
#Text=Liquid aliment is best for them; as fat broths, &c. but low living is bad.
#Text=They should frequently go a little out of the tainted air; and should never suffer themselves to be costive.

#Text=In the West Indies, and on the coast of Guinea, it has been found of great use, for preventing this colic, to wear a piece of flannel round the waist, and to drink an infusion of ginger by way of tea.
#Text=Sundry other kinds of this disease might be mentioned, but too many distinctions would tend only to perplex the reader.
#Text=Those already mentioned are the most material, and should indeed be attended to, as their treatment is very different.

#Text=But even persons who are not in a condition to distinguish very accurately in these matters, may nevertheless be of great service to patients in colics of every kind, by only observing the following general rules, viz.

#Text=To bathe the feet and legs in warm water; to apply bladders filled with warm water, or cloths dipped in it, to the stomach and bowels; to make the patient drink freely of diluting mucilaginous liquors; and to give him an emollient clyster every two or three hours.
#Text=Should these not succeed, the patient ought to be immersed in warm water.
#Text=INFLAMMATION OF THE KIDNEYS.
#Text=CAUSES.
#Text=——This disease may proceed from any of those causes which produce an inflammatory fever.

#Text=It may likewise be occasioned by wounds or bruises of the kidneys; small stones or gravel lodging within them; by strong diuretic medicines; as spirits of turpentine, tincture of cantharides, &c.
#Text=Violent motion, as hard riding or walking, especially in hot weather, or whatever drives the blood too forcibly into the kidneys, may occasion this malady.
#Text=It may likewise proceed from lying too soft, too much on the back, involuntary contractions, or spasms, in the urinary vessels, &c.
#Text=SYMPTOMS.
#Text=——There is a sharp pain about the region of the kidneys, with some degree of fever, and a stupor or dull pain in the thigh of the affected side.

#Text=The urine is at first clear, and afterwards of a reddish colour; but in the worst kind of the disease it generally continues pale, is passed with difficulty, and commonly in small quantities at a time.
#Text=The patient feels great uneasiness when he endeavours to walk or sit upright.
#Text=He lies with most ease on the affected side, and has generally a nausea or vomiting, resembling that which happens in the colic.
#Text=This disease however may be distinguished from the colic by the pain being seated farther back, and by the difficulty of passing urine with which it is constantly attended.
#Text=REGIMEN.
#Text=——Every thing of a heating or stimulating nature is to be avoided.
#Text=The food must be thin and light; as panado, small broths, with mild vegetables, and the like.
#Text=Emollient and thin liquors must be plentifully drank; as clear whey, or balm-tea sweetened with honey, decoctions of marsh-mallow roots, with barley and liquorice, &c.
#Text=The patient, notwithstanding the vomiting, must constantly keep sipping small quantities of these or other diluting liquors.
#Text=Nothing so safely and certainly abates the inflammation, and expels the obstructing cause, as copious dilution.
#Text=The patient must be kept easy, quiet, and free from cold, as long as any symptoms of inflammation remain.
#Text=MEDICINE.
#Text=——Bleeding is generally necessary, especially at the beginning.

#Text=Ten or twelve ounces may be let from the arm or foot with a lancet, and if the pain and inflammation continue, the operation may be repeated in twenty-four hours, especially if the patient be of a full habit.
#Text=Leeches may likewise be applied to the hæmorrhoidal veins, as a discharge from these will greatly relieve the patient.
#Text=Cloths dipped in warm water, or bladders filled with it, must be applied as near as possible to the part affected, and renewed as they grow cool.
#Text=If the bladders be filled with a decoction of mallows and camomile flowers, to which a little saffron is added, and mixed with about a third part of new milk, it will be still more beneficial.
#Text=Emollient clysters ought frequently to be administered; and if these do not open the body, a little salt and honey or manna may be added to them.

#Text=The same course is to be followed where gravel or stone is lodged in the kidney, but when the gravel or stone is separated from the kidney, and lodges in the Ureter[119], it will be proper, besides the fomentations, to rub the small of the back with sweet oil, and to give gentle diuretics; as juniper-water sweetened with the syrup of marsh-mallows: a tea-spoonful of the sweet spirits of nitre, with a few drops of laudanum, may now and then be put in a cup of the patient’s drink.
#Text=He ought likewise to take exercise on horseback, or in a carriage, if he be able to bear it.

#Text=When the disease is protracted beyond the seventh or eighth day, and the patient complains of a stupor and heaviness of the part, has frequent returns of chillness, shivering, &c. there is reason to suspect that matter is forming in the kidney, and that an abscess will ensue.

#Text=When matter in the urine shews that an ulcer is already formed in the kidney, the patient must be careful to abstain from all acrid, sour, and salted provisions; and to live chiefly upon mild mucilaginous herbs and fruits, together with the broth of young animals, made with barley, and common pot-herbs, &c.
#Text=His drink may be whey, and butter-milk that is not sour.
#Text=The latter is by some reckoned a specific remedy in ulcers of the kidneys.
#Text=To answer this character, however, it must be drank for a considerable time.
#Text=Chalybeate waters have likewise been found beneficial in this disease.
#Text=This medicine is easily obtained, as it is found in every part of Great Britain.
#Text=It must likewise be used for a considerable time, in order to produce any salutary effects.

#Text=Those who are liable to frequent returns of inflammation, or obstructions of the kidneys, must abstain from wines, especially such as abound with tartar; and their food ought to be light, and of easy digestion.
#Text=They should use moderate exercise, and should not lie too hot, nor too much on their back.
#Text=INFLAMMATION OF THE BLADDER.
#Text=The inflammation of the bladder proceeds, in a great measure, from the same causes as that of the kidneys.

#Text=It is known by an acute pain towards the bottom of the belly, and difficulty of passing urine, with some degree of fever, a constant inclination to go to stool, and a perpetual desire to make water.
#Text=This disease must be treated on the same principles as the one immediately preceding.
#Text=The diet must be light and thin, and the drink of a cooling nature.
#Text=Bleeding is very proper at the beginning, and in robust constitutions it will often be necessary to repeat it.
#Text=The lower part of the belly should be fomented with warm water, or a decoction of mild vegetables; and emollient clysters ought frequently to be administered, &c.
#Text=The patient should abstain from every thing that is of a hot, acrid, and stimulating quality, and should live entirely upon small broths, gruels, or mild vegetables.

#Text=A stoppage of urine may proceed from other causes besides an inflammation of the bladder; as a swelling of the hæmorrhoidal veins, hard fæces lodged in the rectum; a stone in the bladder; excrescences in the urinary passages, a palsy of the bladder, hysteric affections, &c.
#Text=Each of these requires a particular treatment, which does not fall under our present consideration.
#Text=We shall only observe, that in all of them mild and gentle applications are the safest, as strong diuretic medicines, or things of an irritating nature, generally increase the danger.

#Text=I have known some persons kill themselves by introducing probes into the urinary passages, to remove, as they thought, somewhat that obstructed the discharge of urine, and others bring on a violent inflammation of the bladder, by using strong diuretics, as oil of turpentine, &c. for that purpose.
#Text=INFLAMMATION OF THE LIVER.

#Text=The liver is less subject to inflammation than most of the other viscera, as in it the circulation is slower; but when an inflammation does happen, it is with difficulty removed, and often ends in a suppuration or scirrhus.
#Text=CAUSES.

#Text=——Besides the common causes of inflammation, we may here reckon the following, viz. excessive fatness, a scirrhus of the liver itself, violent shocks from strong vomits when the liver was before unsound, an adust or atrabiliarian state of the blood, any thing that suddenly cools the liver after it has been greatly heated, stones obstructing the course of the bile, drinking strong wines and spirituous liquors, using hot spicy aliment, obstinate hypochondriacal affections, &c.
#Text=SYMPTOMS.

#Text=——This disease is known by a painful tension of the right side under the false ribs, attended with some degree of fever, a sense of weight, or fulness of the part, difficulty of breathing, loathing of food, great thirst, with a pale or yellowish colour of the skin and eyes.
#Text=The symptoms here are various, according to the degree of inflammation, and likewise according to the particular part of the liver where the inflammation happens.

#Text=Sometimes the pain is so inconsiderable, that an inflammation is not so much as suspected; but when it happens in the upper or convex part of the liver, the pain is more acute, the pulse quicker, and the patient is often troubled with a dry cough, a hiccup, and a pain extending to the shoulder, with difficulty of lying on the left side, &c.
#Text=This disease may be distinguished from the pleurisy by the pain being less violent, seated under the false ribs, the pulse not so hard, and by the difficulty of lying on the left side.
#Text=It may be distinguished from the hysteric and hypochondriac disorders by the degree of fever with which it is always attended.
#Text=This disease, if properly treated, is seldom mortal.
#Text=A constant hiccuping, violent fever, and excessive thirst, are bad symptoms.
#Text=If it ends in a suppuration, and the matter cannot be discharged outwardly, the danger is great.

#Text=When the scirrhus of the liver ensues, the patient, if he observes a proper regimen, may nevertheless live a number of years tolerably easy; but if he indulge in animal food and strong liquors, or take medicines of an acrid or irritating nature, the scirrhus will be converted into a cancer, which must infallibly prove fatal.
#Text=REGIMEN.
#Text=——The same regimen is to be observed in this as in other inflammatory disorders.
#Text=All hot things are to be carefully avoided, and cool diluting liquors, as whey, barley-water, &c. drank freely.
#Text=The food must be light and thin, and the body, as well as the mind, kept easy and quiet.
#Text=MEDICINE.
#Text=——Bleeding is proper at the beginning of this disease, and it will often be necessary, even though the pulse should not feel hard, to repeat it.
#Text=All violent purgatives are to be avoided; the body however must be kept gently open.
#Text=A decoction of tamarinds, with a little honey or manna, will answer this purpose very well.
#Text=The side affected must be fomented in the manner directed in the foregoing diseases.
#Text=Mild laxative clysters should be frequently administered; and, if the pain should notwithstanding continue violent, a blistering plaster may be applied over the part affected.
#Text=Medicines which promote the secretion of urine have a very good effect here.
#Text=For this purpose half a drachm of purified nitre, or a tea-spoonful of the sweet spirits of nitre, may be taken in a cup of the patient’s drink three or four times a-day.
#Text=When there is an inclination to sweat, it ought to be promoted, but not by warm sudorifics.
#Text=The only thing to be used for that purpose is plenty of diluting liquors drank about the warmth of the human blood.
#Text=Indeed the patient in this case, as well as in all other topical inflammations, ought to drink nothing that is colder than the blood.
#Text=If the stools should be loose, and even streaked with blood, no means must be used to stop them, unless they be so frequent as to weaken the patient.
#Text=Loose stools often prove critical, and carry off the disease.

#Text=If an abscess or imposthume is formed in the liver, all methods should be tried to make it break and discharge itself outwardly, as fomentations, the application of poultices, ripening cataplasms, &c.
#Text=Sometimes indeed the matter of an abscess comes away in the urine, and sometimes it is discharged by stool, but these are efforts of Nature which no means can promote.

#Text=When the abscess bursts into the cavity of the abdomen at large, death must ensue; nor will the event be more favourable when the abscess is opened by an incision, unless in cases where the liver adheres to the peritonæum, so as to form a bag for the matter, and prevent it from falling into the cavity of the abdomen; in which case opening the abscess by a sufficiently large incision will probably save the patient’s life[120].
#Text=If the disorder, in spite of all endeavours to the contrary, should end in a scirrhus, the patient must be careful to regulate his diet, &c. in such a manner as not to aggravate the disease.

#Text=He must not indulge in flesh, fish, strong liquors, or any highly seasoned or salted provisions; but should, for the most part, live on mild vegetables, as fruits and roots; taking gentle exercise, and drinking whey, barley-water, or butter-milk.
#Text=If he takes any thing stronger, it should be fine mild ale, which is less heating than wines or spirits.
#Text=We shall take no notice of inflammations of the other viscera.
#Text=They must in general be treated upon the same principles as those already mentioned.

#Text=The chief rule with respect to all of them, is to let blood, to avoid every thing that is strong, or of a heating nature, to apply warm fomentations to the part affected, and to cause the patient to drink a sufficient quantity of warm diluting liquors.
#Text=OF VOMITING.
#Text=From the delicate state of children, and the great sensibility of their organs, a vomiting or looseness may be induced by any thing that irritates the nerves of the stomach or intestines.
#Text=Hence these disorders are much more common in childhood than in the more advanced periods of life.
#Text=They are seldom however dangerous, and ought never to be considered as diseases, unless when they are violent, or continue so long as to exhaust the strength of the patient.

#Text=Vomiting may be excited by an over-quantity of food; by food that is of such a nature as to irritate the nerves of the stomach too much; or by the sensibility of the nerves being so much increased as to render them unable to bear the stimulus of even the mildest element.
#Text=When vomiting is occasioned by too much food, it ought to be promoted, as the cure will depend upon cleansing the stomach.
#Text=This may be done either by a few grains of ipecacuanha, or a weak solution of emetic tartar, as mentioned before.
#Text=When it is owing to food of an acrid or irritating quality, the diet ought to be changed, and aliment of a milder nature substituted in its stead.

#Text=When vomiting proceeds from an increased degree of sensibility, or too great an irritability of the nerves of the stomach, such medicines as have a tendency to brace and strengthen that organ, and to abate its sensibility, must be used.

#Text=The first of these intentions may be answered by a slight infusion of the Peruvian bark, with the addition of a little rhubarb and orange-peel; and the second by the saline draughts, to which a few drops of liquid laudanum may be occasionally added.

#Text=In obstinate vomitings the operation of internal medicines may be assisted by aromatic fomentations made with wine, applied warm to the pit of the stomach; or the use of the stomach plaster, with the addition of a little Theriaca.
#Text=CATAPLASMS AND SINAPISMS.
#Text=Cataplasms possess few or no virtues superior to a poultice, which may be so made, as, in most cases, to supply their place.
#Text=They are chiefly intended either to act as discutients, or to promote suppuration; and as they may be of service in some cases, we shall give a specimen of each kind.
#Text=Discutient Cataplasm.
#Text=Take of barley-meal, six ounces; fresh hemlock leaves, bruised; two ounces; vinegar, a sufficient quantity.
#Text=Boil the meal and hemlock in the vinegar for a little, and then add two drachms of the sugar of lead.
#Text=Ripening Cataplasm.

#Text=Take of white lily root, four ounces; fat figs and raw onions, bruised, of each one ounce; yellow basilicum ointment, two ounces; gum galbanum, half an ounce; linseed meal, as much as necessary.
#Text=Boil the roots along with the figs in a sufficient quantity of water; then bruise and add to them the other ingredients, so as to form the whole into a soft cataplasm.
#Text=The galbanum must be previously dissolved with the yolk of an egg.
#Text=Where it is necessary to promote suppuration, this cataplasm may be used by those who chuse to be at the trouble and expence of making it.

#Text=For my part, I have never found any application more proper for this purpose than a poultice of bread and milk, with a sufficient quantity of either boiled or raw onion in it, and softened with oil or fresh butter.
#Text=Sinapisms.
#Text=Sinapisms are employed to recal the blood and spirits to a weak part, as in the palsy and atrophy.
#Text=They are also of service in deep-seated pains, as the sciatica, &c.
#Text=When the gout seizes the head or the stomach, they are applied to the feet to bring the disorder to these parts.
#Text=They are likewise applied to the patient’s soles in the low state of fevers.
#Text=They should not be suffered to lie on, however, till they have raised blisters, but till the parts become red, and will continue so when pressed with the finger.
#Text=The sinapism is only a poultice made with vinegar instead of milk, and rendered warm and stimulating by the addition of mustard, horse-radish, or garlic.
#Text=The common sinapism is made by taking crumb of bread and mustard-seed in powder, of each equal quantities; strong vinegar, as much as is sufficient, and mixing them so as to make a poultice.
#Text=When sinapisms of a more stimulating nature are wanted, a little bruised garlic may be added to the above.
#Text=CLYSTERS.

#Text=This class of medicines is of more importance than is generally imagined. clysters serve, not only to evacuate the contents of the belly, but also to convey very active medicines into the system.
#Text=Opium, for example, may be administered in this way when it will not sit upon the stomach, and also in larger doses than at any time it can be taken by the mouth.
#Text=The Peruvian bark may likewise be, with good effect, administered in form of clyster to persons who cannot take it by the mouth.
#Text=A simple clyster can seldom do hurt, and there are many cases where it may do much good.
#Text=A clyster even of warm water, by serving as a fomentation to the parts, may be of considerable service in inflammations of the bladder, and the lower intestines, &c.
#Text=Some substances, as the smoke of tobacco, may be thrown into the bowels in this way, which cannot by any other means whatever.
#Text=This may be easily effected by means of a pair of hand-bellows, with an apparatus fitted to them for that purpose.
#Text=Nor is the use of clysters confined to medicines.
#Text=Aliment may also be conveyed in this way.
#Text=Persons unable to swallow, have been, for a considerable time, supported by clysters.
#Text=Emollient Clyster.
#Text=Take of linseed-tea and new milk, each six ounces.
#Text=Mix them.
#Text=If fifty or sixty drops of laudanum be added to this, it will supply the place of the Anodyne Clyster.
#Text=Laxative Clyster.
#Text=Take of milk and water, each six ounces; sweet oil or fresh butter, and brown sugar, of each two ounces.
#Text=Mix them.
#Text=If an ounce of Glauber’s salt, or two table-spoonfuls of common salt, be added to this, it will be the Purging Clyster.
#Text=Carminative Clyster.
#Text=Take of camomile flowers, an ounce; anise-seeds, half an ounce.
#Text=Boil in a pint and a half of water to one pint.
#Text=In hysteric and hypochondriac complaints this may be administered instead of the Fœtid Clyster, the smell of which is so disagreeable to most patients.
#Text=Oily Clyster.
#Text=To four ounces of the infusion of camomile flowers, add an equal quantity of Florence oil.
#Text=This clyster is beneficial in bringing off the small worms lodged in the lower parts of the alimentary canal.
#Text=When given to children the quantity must be proportionably lessened.
#Text=Starch Clyster.
#Text=Take jelly of starch, four ounces; linseed oil, half an ounce.
#Text=Liquify the jelly over a gentle fire, and then mix in the oil.
#Text=In the dysentery or bloody flux, this clyster may be administered after every loose stool, to heal the ulcerated intestines and blunt the sharpness of corroding humours.
#Text=Forty or fifty drops of laudanum may be occasionally added; in which case, it will generally supply the place of the Astringent Clyster.
#Text=Turpentine Clyster.
#Text=Take of common decoction, ten ounces; Venice turpentine, dissolved with the yolk of an egg, half an ounce; Florence oil, one ounce.
#Text=Mix them.
#Text=This diuretic clyster is proper in obstructions of the urinary passages, and in colicky complaints, proceeding from gravel.
#Text=Vinegar Clyster.
#Text=This clyster is made by mixing three ounces of vinegar with five of water-gruel.
#Text=It answers all the purposes of a common clyster, with the peculiar advantage of being proper either in inflammatory or putrid disorders, especially in the latter
#Text=.
#Text=☞ We think it unnecessary to give more examples of this class of medicines, as ingredients adapted to any particular intention may be occasionally added to one or other of the above forms.
#Text=COLLYRIA, or EYE-WATERS.
#Text=Eye-waters have been multiplied without number, almost every person pretending to be possessed of some secret preparation for the cure of sore eyes.
#Text=I have examined many of them, and find that they are pretty much alike, the basis of most of them being either alum, vitriol, or lead.

#Text=Their effects evidently are, to brace and restore the tone of the parts; hence they are principally of service in slight inflammations; and in that relaxed state of the parts which is induced by obstinate ones.
#Text=Camphor is commonly added to these compositions; but as it seldom incorporates properly with the water, it can be of little use.
#Text=Boles and other earthy substances, as they do not dissolve in water, are likewise unfit for this purpose.
#Text=Collyrium of Alum.
#Text=Take of alum, half a drachm; agitate it well together with the white of one egg.
#Text=This is the Collyrium of Riverius.
