#Text=A pint of it may be drank daily, or more, if the stomach can bear it.
#Text=It is generally ordered to be taken on an empty stomach, viz. four ounces morning and evening, and the same quantity about two hours after breakfast and dinner.
#Text=SIMPLE DISTILLED WATERS.
#Text=A great number of distilled waters were formerly kept in the shops, and are still retained in some Dispensatories.
#Text=But we consider them chiefly in the light of grateful diluents, suitable vehicles for medicines of greater efficacy, or for rendering disgustful ones more agreeable to the palate and stomach.
#Text=We shall therefore insert only a few of those which are best adapted to these intentions.
#Text=The management of a still being now generally understood, it is needless to spend time in giving directions for that purpose.
#Text=Cinnamon Water.
#Text=Steep one pound of cinnamon bark, bruised, in a gallon and a half of water, and one pint of brandy, for two days; and then distil off one gallon.
#Text=This is an agreeable aromatic water, possessing in a high degree the fragrance and cordial virtues of the spice.
#Text=Pennyroyal Water.
#Text=Take of penny-royal leaves, dried, a pound and a half; water, from a gallon and a half to two gallons.
#Text=Draw off by distillation one gallon.
#Text=This water possesses, in a considerable degree, the smell, taste, and virtues of the plant.
#Text=It is given in mixtures and juleps to hysteric patients.
#Text=An infusion of the herb, in boiling water answers nearly the same purposes.
#Text=Peppermint Water.
#Text=This is made in the same manner as the preceding.
#Text=Spearmint Water.
#Text=This may also be prepared in the same way as the penny-royal water.
#Text=Both these are useful stomachic waters, and will sometimes relieve vomiting, especially when it proceeds from indigestion, or cold viscid phlegm.
#Text=They are likewise useful in some colicky complaints, the gout in the stomach, &c. particularly the peppermint water.
#Text=An infusion of the fresh plant is frequently found to have the same effects as the distilled water.
#Text=Rose Water.
#Text=Take of roses fresh gathered, six pounds; water, two gallons.
#Text=Distil off one gallon.
#Text=This water is principally valued on account of its fine flavour.
#Text=Jamaica Pepper Water.
#Text=Take of Jamaica pepper, half a pound; water, a gallon and a half.
#Text=Distil off one gallon.
#Text=This is a very elegant distilled water, and may in most cases supply the place of the more costly spice waters.
#Text=SPIRITUOUS DISTILLED WATERS.
#Text=Spirituous Cinnamon Water.
#Text=Take of cinnamon bark, one pound; proof spirit, and common water, of each one gallon.
#Text=Steep the cinnamon in the liquor for two days; then distil off one gallon.
#Text=Spirituous Jamaica Pepper Water.
#Text=Take of Jamaica pepper, half a pound; proof spirit, three gallons; water, two gallons.
#Text=Distil off three gallons.
#Text=This is a sufficiently agreeable cordial, and may supply the place of the Aromatic Water.
#Text=WHEYS.
#Text=Alum Whey.
#Text=Boil two drachms of powdered alum in a pint of milk till it is curdled; then strain out the whey.
#Text=This whey is beneficial in an immoderate flow of the menses, and in a diabetes, or excessive discharge of urine.
#Text=The dose is two, three, or four ounces, according as the stomach will bear it, three times a-day.
#Text=If it should occasion vomiting, it may be diluted.
#Text=Mustard-Whey.
#Text=Take milk and water, of each a pint; bruised mustard-seed, an ounce and a half.
#Text=Boil them together till the curd is perfectly separated; afterwards strain the whey through a cloth.
#Text=This is the most elegant, and by no means the least efficacious method of exhibiting mustard.
#Text=It warms and invigorates the habit, and promotes the different secretions.
#Text=Hence, in the low state of nervous fevers, it will often supply the place of wine.
#Text=It is also of use in the chronic rheumatism, palsy, dropsy, &c.
#Text=The addition of a little sugar will render it more agreeable.
#Text=The dose is an ordinary tea-cupful four or five times a-day.
#Text=Scorbutic Whey.
#Text=This whey is made by boiling half a pint of the scorbutic juices in a quart of cow’s milk.
#Text=More benefit, however, is to be expected from eating the plants, than from their expressed juices.
#Text=The scorbutic plants are, bitter oranges, brooklime, garden scurvy-grass, and water-cresses.
#Text=A number of other wheys may be prepared nearly in the same manner, as orange whey, cream of tartar whey, &c.
#Text=These are cooling pleasant drinks in fevers, and may be rendered cordial, when necessary, by the addition of wine.
#Text=WINES.
#Text=The effects of wine are, to raise the pulse, promote perspiration, warm the habit, and exhilarate the spirits.

#Text=The red wines, besides these effects, have an astringent quality, by which they strengthen the tone of the stomach and intestines, and by this means prove serviceable in restraining immoderate secretions.
#Text=The thin sharp wines have a different tendency.
#Text=They pass off freely by the different emunctories, and gently open the body.
#Text=The effects of the full bodied wines are, however, much more durable than those of the thinner.
#Text=All sweet wines contain a glutinous substance, and do not pass off freely.
#Text=Hence they will heat the body more than an equal quantity of any other wine, though it should contain fully as much spirit.
#Text=From the obvious qualities of wine, it must appear to be an excellent cordial medicine.
#Text=Indeed, to say the truth, it is worth all the rest put together.
#Text=But to answer this character, it must be sound and good.
#Text=No benefit is to be expected from the common trash that is often sold by the name of wine, without possessing one drop of the juice of the grape.
#Text=Perhaps no medicine is more rarely obtained genuine than wine.

#Text=Wine is not only used as a medicine, but is also employed as a menstruum for extracting the virtues of other medicinal substances; for which it is not ill adapted, being a compound of water, inflammable spirit, and acid; by which means it is enabled to act upon vegetable and animal substances, and also to dissolve some bodies of the metallic kind, so as to impregnate itself with their virtues, as steel, antimony, &c.
#Text=Anthelmintic Wine.
#Text=Take of rhubarb, half an ounce; worm-seed, an ounce.
#Text=Bruise them, and infuse without heat in two pints of red port wine for a few days, then strain off the wine.

#Text=As the stomachs of persons afflicted with worms are always debilitated, red wine alone will often prove serviceable: it must, however, have still better effects when joined with bitter and purgative ingredients, as in the above form.
#Text=A glass of this wine may be taken twice or thrice a-day.
#Text=Antimonial Wine.
#Text=Take glass of antimony, reduced to a fine powder, half an ounce; Lisbon wine, eight ounces.
#Text=Digest, without heat, for three or four days, now and then shaking the bottle; afterwards filter the wine through paper.
#Text=The dose of this wine varies according to the intention.
#Text=As an alterative and diaphoretic, it may be taken from ten to fifty or sixty drops.
#Text=In a larger dose it generally proves cathartic, or excites vomiting.
#Text=Bitter Wine.
#Text=Take of gentian root, yellow rind of lemon-peel, fresh, each one ounce; long pepper, two drachms; mountain wine, two pints.
#Text=Infuse without heat for a week, and strain out the wine for use.
#Text=In complaints arising from weakness of the stomach, or indigestion, a glass of this wine may be taken an hour before dinner and supper.
#Text=Ipecacuanha Wine.
#Text=Take of ipecacuanha, in powder, one ounce; mountain wine, a pint.
#Text=Infuse for three or four days; then filter the tincture.
#Text=This is a safe vomit, and answers extremely well for such persons as cannot swallow the powder, or whose stomachs are too irritable to bear it.
#Text=The dose is from one ounce to an ounce and a half.
#Text=Chalybeate or Steel Wine.
#Text=Take filings of iron, two ounces; cinnamon and mace, of each two drachms; Rhenish wine, two pints.
#Text=Infuse for three or four weeks, frequently shaking the bottle; then pass the wine through a filter.
#Text=In obstructions of the menses, this preparation of iron may be taken, in the dose of half a wine-glass twice or thrice a-day.
#Text=The medicine would probably be as good if made with Lisbon wine, sharpened with half an ounce of the cream of tartar, or a small quantity of the vitriolic acid.
#Text=Stomach Wine.
#Text=Take of Peruvian bark, grossly powdered, an ounce; cardamom seeds, and orange peel, bruised, of each two drachms.
#Text=Infuse in a bottle of white port or Lisbon wine for five or six days; then drain off the wine.

#Text=This wine is not only of service in debility of the stomach and intestines, but may also be taken as a preventive, by persons liable to the intermittent fever, or who reside in places where this disease prevails.
#Text=It will be of use likewise to those who recover slowly after fevers of any kind, as it assists digestion, and helps to restore the tone and vigour of the system.
#Text=A glass of it may be taken two or three times a-day.
